In last week’s Climate Switch, we covered how the need to rethink urban transportation poses an opportunity not just to reduce emissions but also to make cities more liveable. This week, we’ll turn to two other parts of the transportation sector in need of an overhaul: aviation and shipping.


While the two contribute less to climate change than moving people and stuff around by road, each still accounts for roughly **2 percent of energy-related** CO2 emissions globally, according to the International Energy Agency ([aviation](https://t.dripemail2.com/c/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJhdWQiOiJkZXRvdXIiLCJpc3MiOiJtb25vbGl0aCIsInN1YiI6ImRldG91cl9saW5rIiwiaWF0IjoxNjk2MDA5ODc2LCJuYmYiOjE2OTYwMDk4NzYsImFjY291bnRfaWQiOiIxMjQwNTY4IiwiZGVsaXZlcnlfaWQiOiJ3cTJvbmY0MGl2YXlyaTliOGFobSIsInVybCI6Imh0dHBzOi8vd3d3LmllYS5vcmcvZW5lcmd5LXN5c3RlbS90cmFuc3BvcnQvYXZpYXRpb24_X19zPXo2ODJhNzg4czhnNjcyOHRmZnNxJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9ZHJpcCZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1DbGltYXRlK1N3aXRjaCstK1NlcCsyOSM6fjp0ZXh0PUluJTIwMjAyMiUyMGF2aWF0aW9uJTIwYWNjb3VudGVkJTIwZm9yJTIwMiUyNSUyMG9mJTIwZ2xvYmFsJTIwZW5lcmd5JTJEcmVsYXRlZCUyMENPMiUyMGVtaXNzaW9ucyJ9.ZbmCz24kqwdxpPPdE6tQKh4LkgfvgM5jfT6KhXMrDzE); [shipping](https://t.dripemail2.com/c/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.-OnRleHQ9SW4lMjAyMDIyJTIwaW50ZXJuYXRpb25hbCUyMHNoaXBwaW5nJTIwYWNjb3VudGVkJTIwZm9yJTIwYWJvdXQlMjAyJTI1JTIwb2YlMjBnbG9iYWwlMjBlbmVyZ3klMkRyZWxhdGVkJTIwQ08yJTIwZW1pc3Npb25zIn0.B0dnpMfyEJQQx18rdxSNOuOjnSwoXWmtEXreBBoF1NM)). And decarbonizing them isn’t quite as straightforward as it’s proving with cars and swapping out internal combustion engines for batteries. So what can we do?


Well, in some cases, batteries are still a solid bet.  
Rapidly declining battery costs have made the electrification of ships plying routes of less than 1,500 km [the most economical option](https://t.dripemail2.com/c/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..E_pBdfvByHu0-u_EzBx8CX6Y3JhItCbDh7UAmjcCcq4). As of July, [China](https://t.dripemail2.com/c/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.-OnRleHQ9Q2hpbmElMjBoYXMlMjBsYXVuY2hlZCUyMGl0cyUyMGZpcnN0LGNhbiUyMGJlJTIwZXhjaGFuZ2VkJTIwZW4lMjByb3V0ZS4ifQ.6wKXaqRM9lf3GcNn8hKl4NnXX8Cn0u6vc9ry7rQ920A) has launched a ship with room for 700 shipping containers; it runs on batteries that can be swapped out as it travels hundreds of miles up the Yangtze River. 



Another upstart shipping solution—one that’s only been around for a few millennia—is [wind power](https://t.dripemail2.com/c/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..LOZhkRERoIojHPelVGeOb9HhEJK7Ri5G0uWLQLMCqc8). It’s also worth noting a lot less shipping may be necessary in a future free of fossil fuels, which accounted for [almost 40%](https://t.dripemail2.com/c/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..MY10UmUHcRqatbJidMwPHoM1YAGKqcM46oc8gvPmZ5Y) of total shipping in 2017. See [[Powering Ships with a kite]]

 
One other big part of the answer may also be hydrogen storage. Briefly, provided a long list of puzzle pieces come together the right way for the climate, the process of hydrolysis uses solar or wind energy to split water into hydrogen and oxygen. Liquefying and storing the hydrogen then affords you a way to later [power a fuel cell](https://t.dripemail2.com/c/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJhdWQiOiJkZXRvdXIiLCJpc3MiOiJtb25vbGl0aCIsInN1YiI6ImRldG91cl9saW5rIiwiaWF0IjoxNjk2MDA5ODc2LCJuYmYiOjE2OTYwMDk4NzYsImFjY291bnRfaWQiOiIxMjQwNTY4IiwiZGVsaXZlcnlfaWQiOiJ3cTJvbmY0MGl2YXlyaTliOGFobSIsInVybCI6Imh0dHBzOi8vd3d3LmZjaGVhLm9yZy9mdWVsY2VsbHM_X19zPXo2ODJhNzg4czhnNjcyOHRmZnNxJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9ZHJpcCZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1DbGltYXRlK1N3aXRjaCstK1NlcCsyOSJ9.2ad_nE3p91sfNQzc8WWfRdILo2ejW6AkC0r4QoDtEZI) and generate electricity.

 
Both hydrogen and batteries may have a role in decarbonizing not just shipping but aviation, as well. In March, a 40-passenger hydrogen electric plane took a successful [maiden flight](https://t.dripemail2.com/c/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..gJeVZK9MKghUca0Wfi5QZ_dqKG2xyghaPAlCNEnnZTc), but it will still be a few years before you find your seat aboard such a machine.

See [[Sustainable FLighs with Hydrogen]]
 
In the meantime, many airlines are turning to SAFs, or “sustainable aviation fuel,” a “drop-in” replacement that [Canary Media](https://t.dripemail2.com/c/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9._X19zPXo2ODJhNzg4czhnNjcyOHRmZnNxJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9ZHJpcCZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1DbGltYXRlK1N3aXRjaCstK1NlcCsyOSJ9.v3mpBG-8Q_92zMChxrEJKTzrcH5GkAvESbMduUecdFQ) notes can be made from corn stalks or municipal solid waste, but most often derives from used cooking oil. Climate Action 100+ [reported in 2022](https://t.dripemail2.com/c/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9._X19zPXo2ODJhNzg4czhnNjcyOHRmZnNxJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9ZHJpcCZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1DbGltYXRlK1N3aXRjaCstK1NlcCsyOSMifQ.4cKCIjt_aNwABWm_2HnUz0xiwDsleUPHv5bfuKZqZ-c) that “a massive, rapid scale-up of sustainable aviation fuels (SAF)” is crucial to keeping the planet from warming more than 1.5 C since industrialization.
 
 See [[About Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF)]]
 

To that end, one other essential is you. We’re with you, of course—we want to help get you to a meaningful role taking on climate change, whether you travel by ship, plane, or donkey.#
